children.name: Bucco
children.description: Typical puffbirds with stout bodies, large heads, and thick necks that give them a hunched, 'puffed-up' appearance. They are sit-and-wait predators, remaining motionless for long periods before sallying out to catch large insects, lizards, and other small prey.

children.name: Bucco macrodactylus
children.description: Found in the western Amazon basin from Colombia and Ecuador to Bolivia and western Brazil. It has a brown head with a distinctive white band across the nape, a white throat, and a streaked brown and white breast. It inhabits terra firme and vÃ¡rzea forests, often perched low in the understorey.

children.name: Bucco capensis
children.description: A distinctive puffbird of the Amazon basin and Guiana Shield, with a rufous collar, white breast and belly, and a dark cap. Its call is a series of low, mournful whistles that carry through the rainforest. It is one of the most commonly seen puffbirds within its range.

children.name: Bucco noanamae
children.description: A puffbird with sooty cap from the Choco region, Colombia.

children.name: Bucco tamatia
children.description: A spotted puffbird of the Amazon basin.

children.name: Bucco lymbidon
children.description: A puffbird with blue cheek from the southern Amazon.

children.name: Bucco chacuru
children.description: A puffbird with white ear patch from central and eastern South America.

children.name: Chelidoptera
children.description: A monotypic genus of unusual puffbird with a more streamlined silhouette and elongated, swallow-like wings. The Swallow-winged Puffbird is the most aerial of the puffbirds, often seen hawking insects from exposed perches in a manner reminiscent of its namesake swallows.

children.name: Chelidoptera tenebrosa
children.description: A small, dark puffbird found across the Amazon and Orinoco basins, with a unique silhouette among puffbirds. It has a sooty-black body, a white rump and belly, and a distinctive swallow-like shape in flight. It is often seen in family groups perched on exposed branches, from which it makes graceful aerial sallies for insects.

children.name: Monasa
children.description: Black or dark grey puffbirds with a distinctive white or pale bill, known as nunbirds. They are more social than other puffbirds, often foraging in small flocks and uttering a variety of whistled and chattering calls. Their name comes from the Greek monasios, meaning solitary, though they are actually quite gregarious.

children.name: Monasa nigrifrons
children.description: A large nunbird of the Amazon and Orinoco basins, entirely black with a bright orange-red bill and a slight crest on the back of the head. It is often encountered in noisy family groups of 4 to 8 birds along forest edges and riverbanks. Its most common call is a loud, clear whistle.

children.name: Monasa morphoeus
children.description: A nunbird with white bill, from Honduras to Bolivia.

children.name: Monasa flavirostris
children.description: A nunbird with yellow bill from the western Amazon.

children.name: Monasa atra
children.description: A black nunbird of the Guiana Shield.

children.name: Notharchus
children.description: Medium to large puffbirds with heavy bills from Central and South America.

children.name: Notharchus hyperrhynchus
children.description: A large puffbird with white throat, from Mexico to Bolivia.

children.name: Notharchus macrorhynchos
children.description: A puffbird of the Guiana Shield.

children.name: Notharchus swainsoni
children.description: A puffbird of the Atlantic Forest in Brazil.

children.name: Notharchus tectus
children.description: A small pied puffbird from Costa Rica to Bolivia.

children.name: Notharchus ordii
children.description: A puffbird with brown breast band from the Amazon.
